articles.title: Amazon will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk
articles.summary: Amazon's Mechanical Turk, a crowdsourcing service launched in 2005, will stop accepting new customers on July 30, 2026, though existing customers can continue using it. The service, which paid people for simple tasks like CAPTCHA completion and data annotation for AI training, has faced challenges including ethical debates, bot usage, and fraud. This decision effectively puts the service on life support, with no new features planned.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprises that relied on Mechanical Turk for data annotation and other human-in-the-loop tasks will need to find alternative solutions for future projects, while current users can continue but should be aware of the platform's declining investment and potential future shutdown. The platform's struggles with bots and AI-generated work highlight the ongoing challenge of ensuring data quality in crowdsourced annotation for AI model development.
articles.beginner: Mechanical Turk was a service where people did small, simple tasks online for companies, often to help train AI, but it's now closing to new users.
articles.advanced: Given the cessation of new customer intake and lack of new features for Mechanical Turk, enterprises should audit their reliance on the platform for data annotation and explore alternative, more robust human-in-the-loop or synthetic data generation solutions to mitigate future operational risks and ensure data quality for AI model training.

articles.title: New Google commercial imagines a Declaration of Independence written with help from AI
articles.summary: A new Google commercial imagines the Founding Fathers using Google Workspace and AI tools like Gemini to draft the Declaration of Independence. The ad, set 250 years after the original signing, depicts a collaborative process involving Google Docs, Calendar, Meet, and e-signatures, with AI assisting in tasks like visualizing national seal designs and taking meeting notes. While the ad is largely tongue-in-cheek and discreet in its AI evangelism, some critics on social media found it "cringey" and "tone deaf," particularly regarding the limited actual use of AI.
articles.enterpriseAngle: This commercial highlights how AI-powered collaboration tools, even in a humorous historical context, can streamline document creation, scheduling, and communication within an organization, potentially influencing enterprise product development to integrate more AI features into productivity suites. Enterprise security teams should note the emphasis on e-signatures and document access requests, underscoring the importance of robust identity and access management in collaborative environments.
articles.beginner: AI can help with everyday tasks like taking notes during meetings and suggesting visual ideas, making group projects easier.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the integration of AI-powered features within your organization's collaboration suite, focusing on practical applications like automated note-taking, visualization assistance, and secure document access controls to enhance productivity and streamline workflows.

articles.title: Midjourney wants Hollywood studios to reveal the details of their AI usage
articles.summary: AI startup Midjourney is in a legal battle with Disney, Universal, and Warner Bros. over alleged copyright infringement, with the studios claiming Midjourney's AI models generate copyrighted characters. Midjourney argues its use falls under fair use and is now pushing for the studios to disclose their own internal AI usage, including prompts and outputs, to support its defense.
articles.enterpriseAngle: This case highlights the growing legal complexities for enterprises developing or using generative AI, particularly concerning intellectual property and fair use, and underscores the need for clear internal policies on AI content generation and data sourcing.
articles.beginner: Companies that create AI tools are being sued by movie studios because the AI can make pictures of copyrighted characters, and the AI company wants to show that the studios might be doing similar things with AI themselves.
articles.advanced: Legal teams should closely monitor the discovery phase of this litigation for precedents regarding the scope of internal AI usage disclosure and its implications for fair use arguments in copyright infringement cases.

articles.title: Alibaba reportedly bans employees from using Claude Code
articles.summary: Alibaba has reportedly banned its employees from using Anthropic's programming tool, Claude Code, starting July 10. This decision follows Anthropic's existing policy prohibiting Chinese companies from using its models and efforts to close loopholes allowing access. Alibaba has classified Claude Code as high-risk software and is directing employees to use its internal Qoder tool.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security teams must be vigilant about unauthorized AI tool usage, especially when geopolitical restrictions or data privacy concerns exist, and should provide secure, compliant internal alternatives. Product development teams should be aware of the risks of model distillation and actively implement stronger mitigations to protect their intellectual property.
articles.beginner: Companies can restrict which AI tools their employees use to ensure security and compliance with international rules.
articles.advanced: Implement robust data governance and access control mechanisms for third-party AI tools, while also developing and promoting secure, in-house AI solutions to mitigate risks associated with unauthorized external model usage and potential intellectual property leakage.

articles.title: What is Mistral AI? Everything to know about the OpenAI competitor
articles.summary: Mistral AI, a French company, is gaining attention as an alternative to U.S. AI labs, focusing on deploying its models and agent platform for enterprise customers and governments. The company is experiencing rapid revenue growth, with annual recurring revenue exceeding $400 million and projected to surpass $1 billion this year. Mistral AI is also making significant investments in research and infrastructure, including a €4 billion strategy to build data centers in France and Sweden, and recently acquired infrastructure startup Koyeb.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams can leverage Mistral AI's approach of forward-deployed engineers and custom model building with Forge to integrate AI securely and tailor it to specific use cases, reducing reliance on centralized control. The company's focus on sovereign tech and building a 'true AI cloud' with dedicated data centers in Europe offers an alternative for enterprises concerned about data sovereignty and supply chain security.
articles.beginner: Mistral AI is a European company that helps businesses and governments use AI by providing custom models and support, aiming to offer an alternative to larger U.S. tech companies.
articles.advanced: Evaluate Mistral AI's Forge platform for custom model development using proprietary data and consider their sovereign AI cloud infrastructure for applications requiring enhanced data governance and regional compliance.

articles.title: The only AI glossary you'll need this year
articles.summary: This article provides a glossary of essential AI terms for both newcomers and experts, covering concepts like AGI, AI agents, API endpoints, chain-of-thought reasoning, coding agents, compute, deep learning, and diffusion. It aims to demystify the rapidly evolving language of artificial intelligence, offering plain-English definitions for key concepts.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Understanding these terms is crucial for enterprise security and product development teams to effectively integrate AI, build secure systems, and leverage advanced automation capabilities like AI and coding agents.
articles.beginner: AI is creating many new words, and this guide helps you understand the most important ones, like what an 'AI agent' is (a tool that does tasks for you).
articles.advanced: Leverage chain-of-thought reasoning in your LLM implementations to enhance accuracy in complex logical and coding tasks, and explore the integration of coding agents for autonomous development workflows.

articles.title: The browser wars aren't about search anymore — here are the best alternatives to Chrome and Safari
articles.summary: The browser landscape is evolving beyond traditional search, with new entrants focusing on AI-powered assistants that act on users' behalf. Companies like Perplexity, The Browser Company, Opera, and OpenAI have launched or are developing browsers that can summarize content, perform tasks, and answer questions. Additionally, there's a growing trend of privacy-focused and open-source browsers offering enhanced security and customization.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams should evaluate the implications of AI-powered browsers, as they introduce new vectors for data handling and potential security risks, while also offering opportunities for increased productivity through automated tasks and intelligent assistance.
articles.beginner: Browsers are becoming smarter, acting like personal assistants that can do more than just show you websites, like summarizing emails or helping you shop.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the security posture and data governance implications of integrating AI-agentic browsers into enterprise workflows, focusing on data residency, access controls, and the potential for autonomous task execution.

articles.title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven't progressed as quickly as he'd hoped
articles.summary: Mark Zuckerberg informed Meta staff that the development of AI agents has not advanced as quickly as anticipated, despite significant investments and a corporate restructuring that involved layoffs and reassignments to AI groups. He noted that the expected benefits of the new AI-focused structure have not yet materialized, though he expects improvements within the next three to six months. Meta has invested heavily in AI infrastructure, with projected spending of up to $145 billion this year.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprises investing heavily in AI, particularly in agent development, should manage expectations regarding immediate returns and be prepared for slower-than-anticipated progress, even with substantial financial and workforce reallocations. The challenges faced by a major player like Meta highlight the complexities of integrating AI at scale and the potential for internal friction during such transitions.
articles.beginner: Even big companies like Meta are finding that making AI agents work perfectly takes more time and effort than they initially thought.
articles.advanced: Given Meta's experience, enterprise AI leaders should prioritize realistic roadmaps for agentic AI deployment, focusing on iterative development and measurable milestones rather than expecting rapid, transformative outcomes from initial large-scale investments.

articles.title: Jersey Mike's IPO illustrates how bad the AI hype has become
articles.summary: Jersey Mike's, a sandwich chain, mentioned "AI" 22 times in its IPO documents, despite selling food and not AI software. This highlights the current trend of companies, even those outside the tech sector, incorporating AI into their investor pitches due to high investor interest. The company included a boilerplate AI risk warning, similar to how other food businesses have faced issues with AI tools, such as Starbucks' failed inventory system.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams should be wary of over-hyping AI capabilities in non-AI products, as it can lead to investor skepticism and unrealistic expectations. Security teams should ensure that any actual AI integration, even in seemingly low-risk environments, is robust and thoroughly tested to avoid operational disruptions seen in other food businesses.
articles.beginner: Even companies that don't make AI products are talking about AI to attract investors, showing how popular AI is right now.
articles.advanced: When evaluating investment opportunities, scrutinize the actual integration and impact of AI mentioned in S-1 filings, particularly for non-tech companies, to differentiate genuine strategic advantage from mere buzzword compliance.

articles.title: Meta quietly launches vibe-coded gaming app Pocket
articles.summary: Meta has quietly launched a new gaming app called Pocket, which allows users to generate small, interactive games and experiences using AI prompts. The app, which resulted from Meta's acquisition of the Gizmo team, also features a scrollable feed to discover user-created content. Pocket appears to be an extension of Meta's broader strategy to integrate AI creation tools into mainstream applications.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams can observe Meta's strategy for integrating AI-powered content generation into consumer applications, potentially informing future approaches to user-generated content and interactive experiences. Security teams should note the inherent risks and governance challenges associated with user-generated AI content, especially in public-facing platforms.
articles.beginner: Meta has a new app where you can use simple text commands to create and play small games and interactive experiences.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the architecture and moderation strategies employed by Meta in Pocket for managing user-generated AI content, and consider their applicability to your own enterprise's AI product development and content governance frameworks.

articles.title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
articles.summary: Anthropic is reportedly in discussions with Samsung to develop a custom AI chip, following earlier reports of their interest in producing their own chips to address shortages. While the specifics of the chip's use and power are undecided, this move aligns with other AI companies seeking custom hardware for specific tasks and greater independence from Nvidia. Anthropic stated that a diversified hardware strategy, including chips from Google, Amazon, and Nvidia, remains crucial.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams reliant on AI models may see increased hardware diversity and potentially more optimized solutions as AI companies invest in custom chips, which could lead to better performance-per-watt and reduced dependency on single vendors. Security teams should consider the implications of a more fragmented hardware landscape on supply chain security and vulnerability management.
articles.beginner: Large AI companies are exploring making their own specialized computer chips to run their AI programs better and reduce reliance on one main chip supplier.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the long-term cost and performance benefits of integrating custom AI silicon into your infrastructure, considering the potential for improved efficiency and reduced vendor lock-in, while also assessing the supply chain risks associated with new hardware partnerships.

articles.title: Achieving operational excellence with AI
articles.summary: The article discusses how frameworks such as Lean Six Sigma and business process management (BPM) provided structured approaches to manage complex operational processes. These frameworks focused on statistical rigor, quality control, and mapping work flows across departments to achieve operational clarity.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprises can leverage AI to enhance existing operational excellence frameworks like Lean Six Sigma and BPM, automating data analysis and process mapping for improved efficiency and quality control.
articles.beginner: AI can help businesses make their operations smoother and more organized, similar to how older methods did but with more power.
articles.advanced: Integrate AI-powered process mining tools with existing Lean Six Sigma and BPM methodologies to identify bottlenecks and optimize workflows in real-time.

articles.title: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund
articles.summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has reportedly suggested donating 5% of the company's equity to a U.S. sovereign wealth fund. This proposal aims to allow the public to share in the financial benefits of the growing AI industry.
articles.enterpriseAngle: This proposal highlights a potential future where AI companies might be pressured to share profits with the public, impacting enterprise valuation and investment strategies. Enterprise leaders should monitor such policy discussions as they could influence the regulatory landscape and public perception of AI development.
articles.beginner: OpenAI wants to give a part of its company to a U.S. fund so that everyday people can benefit financially from the success of AI.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the implications of potential government equity stakes in AI firms on future M&A activities, regulatory frameworks, and public-private partnerships within the AI sector.

articles.title: Microsoft launches its own AI deployment company with $2.5 billion commitment
articles.summary: Microsoft has launched a new operating business called Microsoft Frontier Company, committing $2.5 billion and 6,000 experts to help enterprises successfully deploy its AI tools. This initiative aims to provide outcome-driven engineering support, distinguishing itself from traditional Forward Deployed Engineer models. The venture will leverage Microsoft's existing client relationships, including early partnerships with the London Stock Exchange Group, Unilever, Land O'Lakes, and Accenture.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams can expect more direct, specialized support for integrating Microsoft's AI tools into their operations, potentially accelerating AI adoption and improving implementation success rates. This move signals a growing trend among major tech companies to offer dedicated deployment services, which could streamline complex AI integrations for businesses.
articles.beginner: Microsoft is creating a new company with a lot of money and experts to help other businesses use its AI technologies more effectively.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the potential for Microsoft Frontier Company to streamline complex AI deployments and consider how its outcome-driven engineering approach might differentiate it from other FDE-based AI ventures in your enterprise AI strategy.

articles.title: Teaching AI to run with the turbines
articles.summary: AI is being increasingly used in industrial settings, moving beyond consumer applications like chatbots and image generators. Its most significant applications are found in industries that prioritize physical infrastructure, continuous operation, and safety. In these sectors, AI is becoming a fundamental part of how systems operate.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams should focus on integrating AI into critical infrastructure management, prioritizing robust security protocols and reliability for operational continuity. This shift necessitates developing AI solutions that enhance safety and efficiency in industrial environments.
articles.beginner: AI is being used to help run big industrial machines and systems more safely and efficiently, not just for fun apps.
articles.advanced: Experienced practitioners should prioritize developing and implementing AI solutions that integrate seamlessly with existing industrial control systems, focusing on real-time data processing, predictive maintenance, and anomaly detection to enhance operational resilience and minimize downtime.

articles.title: Yep, we're using OpenClaw to date now
articles.summary: Individuals are using AI agents like OpenClaw and Claude to automate aspects of their dating lives, from generating social media content to planning dates and even sending breakup messages. While some use these tools for efficiency, others employ them in more unconventional ways, such as mass-producing Instagram reels to attract DMs. This trend highlights both the creative potential and the ethical considerations of integrating AI into personal interactions.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams must consider the privacy implications of AI agents having access to personal accounts and data, especially as these tools become more integrated into daily life. The rise of AI agents for personal tasks also presents opportunities for developing secure, human-in-the-loop AI solutions that prioritize user consent and data protection.
articles.beginner: AI agents are computer programs that can perform tasks for you, and people are now using them to help with dating, like making social media posts or finding places to go on dates.
articles.advanced: Implement robust human-in-the-loop approval mechanisms for AI agents accessing personal data and accounts to mitigate privacy risks and prevent unauthorized actions, especially in applications involving sensitive personal interactions.

articles.title: Indian tech tycoon bets $30M of his own money to build AI alternative to Microsoft Office
articles.summary: Indian entrepreneur Bhavin Turakhia has invested $30 million of his own money into Neo, a new enterprise AI platform designed to be an alternative to Microsoft Office. Neo integrates project management, documents, file storage, and AI from the ground up, aiming to make AI an active participant in daily work. Turakhia believes existing workplace software cannot simply be upgraded with AI and requires a complete redesign.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams should consider the implications of AI-native platforms that are model-agnostic, offering flexibility in AI model selection but also requiring robust strategies for managing data privacy and security across different AI providers. The shift towards purpose-built AI solutions suggests a need to evaluate whether current systems can truly integrate AI effectively or if a ground-up redesign is more beneficial for future-proofing.
articles.beginner: New AI tools are being built from scratch to fully use AI's power, rather than just adding AI features to old software.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the long-term viability and security implications of integrating AI into legacy enterprise systems versus adopting AI-native platforms that offer model agnosticism and a unified workflow.

articles.title: More details on Fable 5’s cyber safeguards and our jailbreak framework
articles.summary: Anthropic has redeployed Fable 5 globally, providing more details on its cybersecurity safeguards and an early draft of its AI jailbreak severity framework. The model uses AI systems called safety classifiers to detect and block dangerous cybersecurity uses, categorizing them into prohibited, high-risk dual use, low-risk dual use, and benign use. Anthropic is also seeking feedback on its jailbreak framework and has launched a HackerOne program for security researchers.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security teams can leverage Fable 5 for defensive purposes like scanning codebases for vulnerabilities, while product development teams should be aware of the dual-use nature of AI capabilities and the importance of robust safeguards to prevent misuse. The proposed jailbreak severity framework could help enterprises and governments standardize communication about AI risks.
articles.beginner: AI models like Fable 5 have built-in safety features to prevent them from being used for harmful cybersecurity activities, but these features are still being refined.
articles.advanced: Review Anthropic's proposed AI jailbreak severity framework and consider participating in their HackerOne program to contribute to establishing industry standards for AI security and responsible dual-use capability management.

articles.title: SpaceX has an AI device prototype, and it sure sounds phone-ish
articles.summary: SpaceX reportedly showed investors a prototype of a slim, handset-like AI device, though Elon Musk has denied these reports. This device, if it materializes, could run on a proprietary OS and integrate xAI technology, similar to an AI device OpenAI is developing. The market for such AI devices is currently unproven, with previous attempts by other companies facing challenges.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams should observe the evolving landscape of dedicated AI devices, considering the potential for new form factors and proprietary operating systems to bypass existing platform dependencies. Security teams will need to assess the unique risks and data handling implications of these new AI-native devices if they gain traction.
articles.beginner: Companies like SpaceX and OpenAI are exploring new types of handheld devices that are built specifically for artificial intelligence, rather than general computing.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the strategic implications of proprietary AI device operating systems for platform lock-in and data sovereignty, and consider potential integration points for enterprise-specific AI agents.

articles.title: Ashton Kutcher leaving Sound Ventures to launch new VC firm with Morgan Beller
articles.summary: Ashton Kutcher is departing Sound Ventures to co-found a new venture capital firm with Morgan Beller. While Sound Ventures focused on leading AI labs, Kutcher's new firm will target the underlying infrastructure and energy supporting these AI companies.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise infrastructure and energy providers should note the increasing investor interest in foundational AI components, signaling potential growth and partnership opportunities. Product development teams should consider how their offerings align with the evolving needs of AI infrastructure.
articles.beginner: A new investment firm is being created to fund the essential technology and power that makes AI work.
articles.advanced: Evaluate your current infrastructure investments and energy consumption strategies to ensure alignment with emerging AI foundational layer trends and potential future funding opportunities.

articles.title: The latest AI news we announced in June 2026
articles.summary: Google announced several AI updates in June 2026, including Gemini 3.5 Live Translate for real-time speech translation and new features in Android 17. They also released Gemma 4 12B, an open model that runs locally on laptops, and introduced updates to Gemini 3.5 Flash for building custom agents. Additionally, new tools like Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash were launched for developers, alongside enhancements to Google Finance and NotebookLM.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprises can leverage Gemini 3.5 Flash for improved long-horizon and enterprise automation tasks, such as continuous software testing, and utilize Gemini Omni Flash APIs for building custom, dynamic video workflows. The local execution of Gemma 4 12B offers advanced reasoning and private workflows on everyday hardware, benefiting security-conscious product development teams.
articles.beginner: Google is making everyday devices and apps smarter with new AI features like better translation and AI assistants that understand you more naturally.
articles.advanced: Integrate Gemini 3.5 Flash for enhanced enterprise automation and explore Gemini Omni Flash APIs for multimodal video workflows, while considering Gemma 4 12B for local, private AI processing in your applications.

articles.title: Cloudflare's new policy pushes AI companies to pay for publishers' content
articles.summary: Cloudflare is implementing a new policy by September 15, 2026, to differentiate web crawlers used for traditional search from those used for AI agents and training. By default, "mixed-use" crawlers will be blocked from ad-hosting pages for new and free Cloudflare customers, aiming to give publishers more control over their content and intellectual property. This move encourages AI companies to pay for content use, with initial partnerships including Ceramic.ai and You.com.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams in AI companies must adapt their crawling strategies to comply with Cloudflare's new policy, potentially requiring separate crawlers for search and AI training to avoid content access issues. This shift also presents opportunities for enterprises to monetize their content through new "Pay Per Use" models, impacting data acquisition costs and content licensing strategies.
articles.beginner: Cloudflare is making it harder for AI programs to use website content for free, helping websites get paid for their information.
articles.advanced: Implement distinct crawling agents for search indexing and AI model training/agentic services to ensure compliance with Cloudflare's new default blocking policy and explore integration with 'Pay Per Use' models to monetize proprietary content.

articles.title: New York City educators and industry leaders gathered at Google’s offices to shape the future of AI in classrooms.
articles.summary: Google, the New York Jobs CEO Council, and Urban Assembly hosted an AI summit for 150 education and industry leaders in New York City. The event focused on integrating AI into classrooms to prepare students for future careers, emphasizing problem-solving and essential human skills. Attendees explored tools like Google AI mode and NotebookLM, while stressing the importance of privacy and equitable access.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprises should collaborate directly with educational institutions to shape AI curricula, ensuring future workforces possess both technical AI literacy and critical human skills like adaptability and collaboration. Prioritizing privacy and equitable access in AI tool development for education is crucial for responsible adoption.
articles.beginner: AI tools can help students learn problem-solving, but human skills like teamwork and critical thinking remain very important.
articles.advanced: Actively engage with educational bodies to co-develop AI literacy programs and integrate ethical AI considerations, focusing on practical problem-solving applications and the cultivation of essential soft skills.

articles.title: LLMs are stuck in a groupthink groove. This startup is trying to get them out.
articles.summary: Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit a "groupthink" phenomenon, where they tend to produce similar, predictable outputs, such as consistently generating the number 7 when asked for a random number between 1 and 10. This behavior suggests a lack of true randomness or diverse reasoning in their responses. A new startup is reportedly working on methods to address this issue and encourage more varied outputs from these AI models.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams should be aware of LLM groupthink, as it can impact the diversity and originality of AI-generated content, potentially leading to predictable or biased outcomes in applications like content creation, customer service, or data analysis. Security teams should consider how predictable AI responses might be exploited or indicate underlying vulnerabilities in model training or data.
articles.beginner: AI models can sometimes give very similar answers to the same questions, showing they might not be as creative or random as we think.
articles.advanced: To mitigate LLM groupthink, explore advanced sampling techniques beyond greedy decoding and consider fine-tuning models on diverse, adversarial datasets to encourage broader response distributions and reduce predictable outputs.

articles.title: Venice AI becomes a unicorn with $65M Series A as its privacy-first AI platform takes off
articles.summary: Venice AI, a platform offering private access to over 200 AI models, has secured $65 million in Series A funding, valuing the company at $1 billion. The company emphasizes user privacy by encrypting input client-side and not storing user data, and it hosts "uncensored" open-source models while routing queries to others. Venice AI serves over 3 million active users and has annualized revenues exceeding $70 million.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams should note Venice AI's success in meeting demand for privacy-preserving AI access, highlighting a critical market need for secure and uncensored AI tools that maintain user agency. This approach could influence future product design and security protocols for AI integration within organizations.
articles.beginner: Venice AI is a company that lets people use many different AI tools privately, without their personal information being stored or watched.
articles.advanced: Evaluate Venice AI's client-side encryption and external proxy routing architecture as a potential model for implementing privacy-by-design in enterprise AI applications, particularly for sensitive data processing.

articles.title: Gemini Spark, Google's agentic assistant, is now available on Mac
articles.summary: Google has launched Gemini Spark, an AI agent, for macOS, making it available to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the U.S. This expansion allows Spark to interact with local files and integrate with various third-party applications like Canva, Dropbox, and Instacart. The update also introduces real-time topic tracking and custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) support for personalized app connections.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security teams should evaluate the implications of AI agents accessing local files and third-party applications, while product development teams can leverage custom MCP to build tailored AI assistants for internal workflows.
articles.beginner: AI agents like Gemini Spark can help you organize files, track information, and connect with other apps to automate tasks on your computer.
articles.advanced: Implement robust data governance and access controls for AI agents interacting with sensitive local and cloud data, and explore custom MCP for integrating proprietary enterprise applications.

articles.title: Builders Stage agenda revealed: Practical strategies for scaling startups at TechCrunch Disrupt 2026
articles.summary: The Builders Stage at TechCrunch Disrupt 2026 will offer practical discussions and Q&A sessions for founders, startup operators, and investors. The event aims to provide insights into building and scaling successful companies. Attendees can register to save up to $330.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams can gain insights into startup scaling strategies, potentially informing their own growth and market penetration approaches. Security teams might find value in understanding the operational challenges faced by rapidly scaling companies, which could influence their security architecture and incident response planning.
articles.beginner: TechCrunch Disrupt is an event where people learn how to grow new companies.
articles.advanced: Prioritize attending sessions on operational efficiency and security best practices for rapid scaling to proactively address future enterprise challenges.

articles.title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
articles.summary: Meta is reportedly planning to launch a cloud infrastructure business, offering access to its AI compute power and models. This initiative would position Meta as a competitor to established cloud providers such as Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams could gain new options for accessing AI compute and models, potentially diversifying their infrastructure choices and fostering competitive pricing among cloud providers.
articles.beginner: Meta wants to sell its extra AI computer power and AI programs to other companies, similar to how big tech companies already offer these services.
articles.advanced: Evaluate Meta's forthcoming cloud offerings for cost-effectiveness and performance against incumbent providers to optimize AI infrastructure spend and model deployment strategies.

articles.title: The ‘Father of the Internet' is finally retiring
articles.summary: Vinton Cerf, widely recognized as a "Father of the Internet" for his work on TCP/IP protocols, is retiring from his role as Google's chief internet evangelist after more than 20 years. Cerf predicts that the rise of AI agents will necessitate new, formal interoperability standards, moving away from natural language for precise inter-agent communication. He emphasized that companies defining these standards early could gain significant influence over the future "agentic economy."
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise product development teams should anticipate the need for formal, standardized protocols for AI agent interoperability, as Cerf suggests this will be crucial for composability and precision in multi-agent systems. Security teams should also consider the implications of these new standards on data exchange and trust between autonomous agents.
articles.beginner: The person who helped create the fundamental rules for how computers talk to each other on the internet believes that future AI programs will also need clear, formal rules to communicate effectively and avoid misunderstandings.
articles.advanced: Given Cerf's prediction regarding the necessity of formal interoperability standards for AI agents, experienced practitioners should actively engage in or monitor the development of these emerging protocols to strategically position their enterprise's AI infrastructure and product offerings.

articles.title: Trump drops restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models
articles.summary: The U.S. government has lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's advanced AI models, Mythos and Fable, which were previously unavailable to the public due to licensing requirements. This decision follows weeks of negotiations and pressure to allow American AI companies to compete globally, especially as Asian firms release comparable models. Anthropic has agreed to proactively address security risks and collaborate with the U.S. government on future model releases and protocols.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security teams should note Anthropic's commitment to proactive security risk detection and collaboration with the U.S. government on protocols, which could set precedents for future AI model governance and impact how enterprises deploy and secure advanced AI. Product development teams can now anticipate broader access to these advanced models, potentially accelerating innovation but also requiring careful consideration of evolving compliance and security standards.
articles.beginner: The government is now allowing a company called Anthropic to share its powerful AI models, Mythos and Fable, with more people and countries, after previously restricting them.
articles.advanced: Leverage the renewed public access to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models for competitive analysis and integration planning, while closely monitoring the evolving U.S. government protocols and standards for AI model releases to ensure compliance and mitigate security risks.

articles.title: Wayve launches $85M employee tender offer at $8.5B valuation
articles.summary: Wayve, a UK-based self-driving technology startup, has launched an $85 million tender offer, allowing employees to sell vested equity at an $8.5 billion valuation. This marks their second employee liquidity event and follows a $1.2 billion Series D funding round in February. The company is developing self-learning autonomous driving software and plans robotaxi pilots with Uber and integration into Nissan's driver-assist systems.
articles.enterpriseAngle: This trend of AI startups offering employee tender offers provides a critical retention mechanism for enterprise AI talent, ensuring skilled personnel remain with companies developing advanced AI solutions rather than seeking immediate exits. For product development, Wayve's self-learning approach to autonomous driving, which learns from data rather than relying on pre-built maps, could influence future AI system designs that prioritize adaptability and real-world learning.
articles.beginner: Wayve is a company that teaches cars to drive themselves using AI, and they are letting their employees sell some of their company shares, which is a way to keep good people working there.
articles.advanced: Evaluate the strategic implications of early employee liquidity events via tender offers in high-growth AI sectors, considering their impact on long-term talent retention and the competitive landscape for AI product development.

articles.title: Jul 1, 2026 Introducing the Voice Agent Builder Jul 1, 2026 Introducing the Voice Agent Builder Create a personalized voice agent in under 2 minutes without a single line of code. Read More
articles.summary: xAI has launched Voice Agent Builder, a no-code platform enabling users to create personalized voice agents in under two minutes. This platform integrates telephony, knowledge retrieval, tools, and guardrails, offering a unified speech-to-speech path for production voice agents on Grok Voice. Grok Voice was trained on challenging real-world calls and outperforms competitors like Gemini 3.1 Flash Live and GPT Realtime 1.5 on the "τ-voice Bench" benchmark.
articles.enterpriseAngle: Enterprise security and product development teams can leverage this platform to rapidly deploy high-volume voice agents for customer support, sales, and internal operations, reducing development time and complexity while maintaining control over data and actions through guardrails and API integrations. The unified architecture and training on difficult calls suggest improved reliability and performance for critical business functions.
articles.beginner: This new tool lets you easily create AI voice assistants for your business without needing to write any computer code.

articles.title: Anthropic opens Seoul office
articles.contentText: Announcements Anthropic opens Seoul office and announces new partnerships across the Korean AI ecosystem Jun 17, 2026 We’ve just opened our Seoul office . Alongside it, we’re announcing new partnerships across the Korean AI ecosystem, with the enterprises, startups, and researchers behind some of the most ambitious uses of Claude. We’ve also signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Korea’s Ministry of Science and ICT to advance AI safety. “What I see in Korea are teams who understand that innovation and safety are two sides of the same coin,” said KiYoung Choi, Representative Director of Korea at Anthropic. “Korean organizations are building with Claude to bring the benefits of AI to millions around the world. Opening an office in Seoul gives a long-term home to our work alongside the people shaping Korean leadership in AI.” This week, senior leaders from Anthropic traveled to Seoul to open the office and meet with partners, customers, and developers building with Claude. Supporting Korea’s AI ambitions Anthropic has signed an MOU with Korea’s Ministry of Science and ICT to support the safe and responsible adoption of AI across the public sector. Together, we’ll collaborate on AI safety and cybersecurity, including evaluating model safety in the Korean language with the Korea AI Safety Institute, and exchanging information on AI-enabled cyber threats. Expanding our work with enterprises and startups From WRTN to Law&Company , Korean organizations have been working with Claude for years. Today, we are sharing new examples of how Claude is running across the Korean economy. Some of the deepest adoption is happening among developers. NAVER, a leader in cloud and AI innovation in Asia, has recently deployed Claude Code across its entire engineering organization. Thousands of NAVER engineers are now using Claude Code to diversify their coding tools and maximize coding productivity. At global online game company Nexon, engineering teams use Claude Code to write, review, and ship code for live-service games played by millions around the world. Korea’s largest business groups are also deploying Claude. LG CNS, the IT services arm of LG Group, is rolling out Claude to thousands of employees, who are using it to develop software and deliver technology solutions for clients. It will also deploy Claude across LG Group. Hanwha Solutions—the energy, chemicals, and advanced materials arm of Hanwha—is bringing Claude to global employees through AWS Bedrock, meeting strict in-region data-residency and security requirements. Samsung SDS, the IT services arm of Samsung Group, is deploying Claude to employees across Samsung Electronics. Teams are using Claude—including Claude Cowork and Claude Code—to advance day-to-day knowledge work, agentic workflows, and software development at scale. And startups are building Claude directly into their products. Channel Corp uses Claude to power Channel Talk, its customer AI platform that resolves customer inquiries and analyzes service and sales data to deliver business insights—used by over 230,000 companies across Korea, Japan, and the United States. Supporting research and beneficial deployments Our work in Korea also reaches beyond the private sector. To deepen ties with the academic research community, we will work with the National AI Research Lab (NAIRL), a consortium spanning KAIST, Korea University, Yonsei University, and POSTECH. Anthropic will provide Claude access to up to 60 NAIRL-affiliated researchers, supporting work on AI safety, model evaluation, alignment, robustness, and broader frontier AI research. In the nonprofit sector, Good Neighbors Korea—a child rights specialist NGO—is deploying Claude to help staff analyze program outcomes, navigate social welfare law and internal guidelines, and cut down on the administrative work that keeps them from the field. “Good Neighbors Korea is exploring how responsible AI transformation can support frontline social workers, protect vulnerable populations, and strengthen service delivery,” said Jeongsun Park, Chief Administrative Officer, Good Neighbors Korea. “As we deploy Claude across our organization, we expect the efficiency gains to free our staff from administrative workload so they can focus more on what matters most: serving vulnerable children and communities.” Building with the developer community According to our latest Economic Index , Korea is among the top dozen countries in the world for Claude.ai use, much of it concentrated in technical and creative work. It is also home to one of our most active developer communities, with startups building for global markets from day one. To support them, Claude for Startups is live in Korea, and Claude Meetups have drawn hundreds of Korean developers since September 2025. This week, we co-hosted Claude Build Day with BASS Ventures, bringing together more than 100 Korean founders and developers with engineering, product, and startup leaders from Anthropic for an afternoon of hands-on building. We will also co-host a Push to Prod hackathon with Replit, Korea Investment Partners, and Korea Investment Accelerator. Startup teams will build with Claude Code and get mentorship from Anthropic and Replit engineers. Our Seoul office—led by KiYoung Choi , who brings three decades of experience leading technology businesses across Korea—is now open and hiring across a range of roles. To learn more about joining the team, visit our careers page . Updated June 18 to add details on MOU with the Korean government.

articles.title: Results from first Anthropic Public Record
articles.contentText: Announcements Results from the first Anthropic Public Record Jun 12, 2026 We’re conducting a new survey series, Anthropic Public Record, to understand how the public thinks and feels about AI, and presenting a snapshot of the results from the first wave, fielded in November and December of 2025 with nearly 52,000 Americans. We found: Nearly half (48%) of Americans ranked curing diseases like cancer or Alzheimer’s as one of their top three hopes for AI, followed by helping people with disabilities (36%), then making technological progress and making life easier in general (tied at 23%). AI-induced job loss was the most common fear in every state, held by 64% of Americans. The second most prominent fear was cognitive dependency (56%), followed by misinformation (52%). Support for government intervention in AI was high: over 70% of the Americans we surveyed believe the government should play a role in regulating AI, and this support was bipartisan. People were most eager to see the government take action on AI in the areas of privacy (56%), child safety (52%), and liability for harm (49%). When asked what would best ensure AI is of benefit to humanity, Americans ranked holding AI companies legally liable for harm (47%) and prioritizing safety over growth (44%) as the highest-leverage actions. Only 15% of Americans said they trust AI companies to make decisions about how AI is developed and used. Job-loss worry Integrated users Support for gov involvement Strikingly, on most questions, AI did not heavily divide Americans along typical partisan, geographic, or educational lines. In general, there was broad consensus across topics: Americans are eager to realize AI’s promised benefits but fear the disruption it may bring, and they want accountability from the companies building it. To the extent we saw disagreement, it was largely only in the intensity of people's views. This research builds on other work underway at Anthropic to understand how people use Claude and think about AI development. We recently conducted a global qualitative study of 81,000 Claude users through Anthropic Interviewer , our tool for conducting in-depth interviews at scale. We also regularly release data from the Anthropic Economic Index , which draws on anonymized Claude usage data to show how people around the world are employing AI. The Anthropic Public Record survey marks the first time we’ve spoken to the general public, allowing us to reach non-users of AI and better understand how attitudes differ across demographic lines. The Anthropic Public Record will be repeated regularly, evolving in scope as new topics become more salient, and allowing us to track how the public’s attitudes towards AI change as model capabilities advance and adoption deepens. In the future, we plan to expand outside the US. Method in brief We conducted a nationally representative online survey in November and December of 2025 of 51,993 Americans, sourced from YouGov and weighted to US Census benchmarks. State samples range from n=232 (Alaska) to n=1,902 (New York), with state-level margins of error between ±2.6 and ±9.1 percentage points. More details on the methodology are available in the Appendix. What Americans hope AI will deliver We asked Americans to choose their top three hopes for AI from a list of 17. Curing disease topped the list, with 48% of respondents putting it in their top three, 12 percentage points ahead of the second most commonly selected item, helping people with disabilities, at 36%. Items like therapy and reducing loneliness—or hopes that AI might substitute for human contact—were the lowest ranked of the options presented. What Americans fear We gave respondents a list of 20 possible harms from AI, asked them to flag each one they felt personally concerned by and then to rate each on a five-point scale of how worried they were. We considered any response of 2 (somewhat worried) or higher as worried. (This methodology differs from the question above, in which we asked participants to rank only their three hopes; the numbers aren’t comparable.) Job loss was by far the most common concern, held by nearly two-thirds (64%) of Americans. This was followed by cognitive dependency—in which AI integration leaves people unable to think for themselves—at 56%, and misinformation at 52%. Job loss and cognitive dependency were also among the top fears in our qualitative study of 81,000 people using Claude. The most common harms tended to be near-term and concrete: job loss, cognitive dependency, misinformation, criminal use, and surveillance. Each of these fears also predates AI, having precedent in an earlier technology—for example, automation causing job loss, smartphones fostering dependency, and social media spreading misinformation. In general, Americans tended to be more concerned with the misuse of AI than AI misalignment, citing criminal use, surveillance, and terrorism more frequently than, for example, AI “going rogue”. For all but three of the harms we mentioned, a majority of respondents described themselves as “not worried,” but there was no potential harm about which less than 1/4 of Americans had at least some concern. Patterns with job loss Sixty-four percent of Americans are worried that AI will displace jobs. The concern is remarkably evenly distributed. It is the top-ranked fear among Democrats (67%) and Republicans (62%), in households with children (59%) and without (66%), and in every state from Iowa at the high end (71%) to Mississippi at the low end (57%). Job loss concerns are higher among Americans with more education Concerns over job displacement rise with a respondent’s education level. Americans with postgraduate degrees are nearly 10 percentage points more worried about job loss than those with a high school education or less. The workers most worried about displacement, in other words, are the ones whose work already overlaps more closely with what AI is being asked to do—a finding reflected in our economic research team’s analysis of our global Anthropic Interviewer study. Fear of job loss is heightened among those who use AI least At the same time, people who use AI at work every day are notably less worried about job loss than people who don’t use AI at all: 54% versus 70%. There are many possible explanations for this trend. Hands-on experience with AI may help people develop skills and fluency that allow them to augment rather than automate parts of their job, making job loss seem like less of a looming threat. Hands-on experience may also reveal AI's limitations. Likely it’s a combination of these and other factors. Perceived capabilities and acceptance of AI at work We gave respondents a list of 14 workplace tasks and for each asked two questions: “How well do you think an AI tool or application could perform this task today?” and “Thinking about your own job, how much AI involvement would you prefer for each of the following work tasks/purposes?” Overall asse

articles.title: TCS and Anthropic bring Claude to regulated industries
articles.contentText: Announcements TCS and Anthropic partner to bring Claude to regulated industries Jun 12, 2026 We’re announcing a partnership with Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), one of the world’s largest technology services companies. TCS will provide Claude to 50,000 of its own employees across 56 countries; build Claude-powered products for clients in financial services, healthcare, the public sector, and other regulated industries; and join the Claude Partner Network, our network of consulting and services firms that help bring Claude to their enterprise clients. Regulated industries need their work to be highly accurate and auditable, and enterprises in these fields already use Claude for that reason. TCS brings decades of experience delivering technology that is compliant with companies’ regulatory requirements—and the reach to bring Claude to thousands of enterprises worldwide. How TCS is using Claude As “customer zero,” TCS will put Claude to work across its own engineering, finance, legal, marketing, and sales teams, using what it learns to shape how it brings Claude to clients. It’s also building a dedicated practice for this partnership, bringing together consultants, engineers, and industry specialists who will design and run Claude-based systems for clients. TCS will package Claude into industry-specific offerings, such as claims processing for insurers and lending advisory for banks, and its teams will implement and run them for clients in financial services, public services, life sciences, healthcare, aviation, telecom, and medical technology. This work is already underway. For example: Diligenta , TCS’s UK life and pensions business, will use Claude to improve the customer experience for the more than 22 million policyholders it serves. TCS’s banking and financial services product teams will use Claude Code to boost productivity around software engineering and IT operations. TCS’s engineering teams will add reusable skills and plugins to the Claude Code ecosystem, starting with ones focused on claims adjudication and lending advisory. TCS iON , which conducts more than 75 million assessments each year across 1,500 cities in India, will deliver Claude training and certification. “Enterprise AI value comes from understanding business context, orchestrating complex systems, and applying deep AI engineering talent,” said K. Krithivasan, Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director of TCS. “By combining Claude with our industry expertise, engineering rigor, and large-scale transformation capabilities, we will help customers move faster to production—especially in industries where trust, resilience, and regulatory discipline are critical. This partnership reflects TCS’s broader strategy to help clients become perpetually adaptive enterprises by turning frontier AI into transformation at enterprise scale.” “We built Claude to be safe, trusted, and helpful, particularly in contexts where accuracy matters most,” said Dario Amodei, Co-founder and CEO of Anthropic. “This partnership deepens our commitment to India, our second-largest market, with TCS bringing Claude to enterprises and professionals across the region and globally.” “This partnership reflects our shared conviction that AI will be foundational and transformative for enterprises worldwide,” said N Chandrasekaran, Chairman, Tata Sons. “By combining Anthropic's capabilities with Tata Group’s scale, trusted relationships, and nation-building commitment, we will accelerate enterprise reinvention and equip India’s youth with the skills to lead in the AI era.” To learn more about the Claude Partner Network, visit anthropic.com/partners .

articles.title: Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
articles.contentText: Announcements Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Jun 12, 2026 The US government, citing national security authorities, has issued an export control directive to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees. The net effect of this order is that we must abruptly disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all our customers to ensure compliance. Access to all other Anthropic models will not be affected. We received the directive from the government today at 5:21pm (ET). The letter did not provide specific details of its national security concern. Our understanding is that the government believes it has become aware of a method of bypassing, or “jailbreaking” Fable 5. We reviewed a demonstration of this specific technique being used to identify a small number of previously known, minor vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities all appear relatively simple, and we have found that other publicly-available models are able to discover them as well without requiring a bypass. Anthropic’s posture with respect to Fable’s safeguards, as laid out in our launch blog post , is the following: We have instituted strong safeguards that greatly reduce the likelihood that Fable is misused for tasks related to cybersecurity (among others). In fact, our safeguards are so strong that many users have complained that they are overly broad. In the weeks leading up to the launch of Fable, Anthropic worked with the US government, the UK AISI, multiple private third-party organizations and internal teams to red-team Fable’s safeguards for thousands of hours in total. These tests showed that Fable’s safeguards are substantially more effective than those of any previously deployed model. No testers have yet been able to find a universal jailbreak —a jailbreak method that can very broadly bypass the model’s safeguards, unblocking a wide range of cyber capabilities. We suspect that perfect jailbreak resistance is not currently possible for any model provider. Every safeguard used in the industry is vulnerable to non-universal jailbreaks (which can elicit some cyber information in specific circumstances), and it is likely that universal jailbreaks will eventually be found in the future. We stated this clearly when we released Fable 5. Given that perfect jailbreak resistance does not appear to be possible today, Anthropic adopted a defense in depth strategy with Fable 5. We aimed to make jailbreaks either narrow (in the case of non-universal jailbreaks) or very expensive to produce (in the case of universal jailbreaks), and to combine this with thorough monitoring to quickly detect and shut down any successful attacks. This is also why Anthropic has required 30-day retention of customer data with Fable—a policy change that carries real costs for us with customers , but that allows us to research and mitigate jailbreaks. We stand by this defense in depth strategy. It reduces the risks posed by Fable, making them comparable to the risks of existing models already deployed across the industry. We have not even received a disclosure of a concerning non-universal potential jailbreak that led to a harmful result. The potential jailbreaks that have been disclosed to us are either entirely benign responses or are minor findings that provide no Mythos-specific uplift. To date, the government has only given us verbal evidence of a potential narrow, non-universal jailbreak, which essentially consists of asking the model to read a specific codebase and fix any software flaws. Our understanding is that one potential jailbreak was shared with the government. We have reviewed a report that we believe is the basis of the government's directive and validated that the level of capability displayed there is widely available from other models (including OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 ), and is used every day by the defenders who keep systems safe. We will share more details over the next 24 hours. We are complying with the government’s legal directive and are removing access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all users. However, we disagree that the finding of a narrow potential jailbreak should be cause for recalling a commercial model deployed to hundreds of millions of people. If this standard was applied across the industry, we believe it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers. As we have stated publicly , we believe the government should have the ability to block unsafe deployments, as part of a statutory process that is transparent, fair, clear, and grounded in technical facts. This action does not adhere to those principles. We apologize for this disruption to our customers. We believe this is a misunderstanding and are working to restore access as soon as possible.

articles.title: Our new community investments in Virginia support local jobs and expand energy affordability.
articles.contentText: Breadcrumb Innovation & AI Infrastructure & Cloud Global Network Virginia has been a home for Google for more than a decade, with an office in Reston and data centers in Loudoun and Prince William Counties. Today, we’re deepening our commitment to the Commonwealth with new community investments that will support thousands of local jobs, prepare the next-generation workforce and expand energy affordability. To prepare Virginians for skilled jobs created by infrastructure growth across the state, we’re funding the electrical training ALLIANCE (etA) to support local electrical apprenticeship training facilities. With this funding, they aim to increase training capacity to support an additional 2,741 apprentices by 2030. This builds on our existing local support and is part of a national commitment from Google.org to prepare over 300,000 skilled tradespeople . As we responsibly build data centers in Virginia, we have invested in over 500 megawatts of new energy capacity, collaborating with partners to bring more power to the grid. To support energy affordability at the local level, we're also launching a $15 million Energy Impact Fund. articles.title: Introducing Claude Corps
articles.contentText: Announcements Policy Introducing Claude Corps Jun 11, 2026 We’re launching Claude Corps , a national fellowship program for people early in their careers who are passionate about extending the benefits of AI to communities across America. We’ll teach 1,000 fellows how to use Claude well, match them with nonprofits across America, and pay them to spend a year—full-time, in-person—helping host organizations to advance their missions. Our goals are twofold: that host organizations are equipped with valuable tools and systems, and fellows build AI skills that will serve them in their careers. The benefits of transformative AI systems could come at the cost of significant disruption. The companies building this technology have a responsibility to make sure the benefits are fully realized and widely shared, and to invest directly in the workers absorbing the change. As such, we’re committing an initial $150m to this program. If Claude Corps works, we'll have a foundation for something much larger: a model for widening AI's benefits during a period of vast economic change. We’re announcing Claude Corps alongside our policy framework for addressing AI's impact on work. How Claude Corps works Claude Corps is set up as a partnership between three organizations. Anthropic will fund the program, lead its overall strategy, and provide Claude expertise. CodePath , an Anthropic nonprofit partner and America’s largest provider of collegiate computer science education, will act as the fellows’ official employer of record and lead programming during the fellowship. Social Finance , a nonprofit and registered investment advisor, will lead measurement and evaluation, and will build a longer-term financial vehicle to enable the program to scale. The fellow experience Each fellowship lasts for 12 months. At the beginning of the program, Anthropic and CodePath will provide intensive training on using Claude in nonprofit settings. After being placed, fellows will receive five hours of ongoing training each week, with the remainder of their time dedicated to their host organization. Fellows will receive a full-time salary of $85,000 and benefits, support from a CodePath mentor, office hours from Anthropic for their technical questions, an expansive Claude token budget, and professional guidance from their manager at the host organization. Over the next 12 months, at least 400 nonprofits will host Claude Corps Fellows. Host organizations include: Braven (Chicago, Illinois). A nonprofit teaching first-generation and lower-income students how to land a strong first job. Code the Dream (Durham, North Carolina). A nonprofit that provides free coding education and paid software apprenticeships to people seeking the skills they need to build a better life. Heartland Forward (Bentonville, Arkansas). A nonpartisan think-and-do tank focused on accelerating economic growth in the American heartland. Montgomery County Food Bank (Conroe, Texas). A food bank that feeds children, seniors, and families north of Houston through more than 100 local pantries and partner agencies. Team Red, White & Blue (Floyds Knobs, Indiana). A nonprofit that supports veteran health and wellness through events and programs focused on building a healthy lifestyle. Reef Environmental Education Foundation (Key Largo, Florida). A marine conservation nonprofit that conducts underwater surveys to protect reefs. SoundOff (San Antonio, Texas). A nonprofit that provides anonymous access to licensed counselors and peer support for Service Members . StriveTogether (Cincinnati, Ohio). A national nonprofit that supports local partnerships to change the way their communities work together, using data, to help young people thrive. YMCA of Greater Charlotte (Charlotte, North Carolina). The local Y across the Charlotte region, with 14 Centers, three program facilities, and two overnight camps serving nearly 300,000 kids, families, and seniors a year. You can hear more from our hosts below: In 70 communities across the US, we’re working to put more young people on a path to economic opportunity. That requires constantly tracking, analyzing, and sharing data and on-the-ground insights so the strategies that work in one place can take root everywhere. AI changes that equation. With our Claude Corps fellow, we’ll be able to do analysis to connect the dots at a pace that was unimaginable in the past. This sort of dedicated technical talent enables us to move faster on work that directly benefits the millions of young people we serve. Jennifer Blatz StriveTogether , CEO Goodwill Industries International is participating in Claude Corps to help us bridge the gap between AI's potential and its responsible, real-world application. We look forward to learning from peers, sharing our experience and gaining insights that will drive meaningful impact across our work. Steve Preston President & CEO , Goodwill Industries International Team Red, White & Blue has always innovated with technology to enrich veterans’ lives. AI is reshaping the landscape we operate in, and we’re investing in the tools that will define how we deliver our mission for the next decade. For Team RWB, community is the solution. Claude Corps fellows will help us scale it with data, automation, and personalization. Dan Brostek Senior Director of Technology , Team Red, White & Blue Hunger doesn’t wait, and neither can innovation. Food banks need to be as forward-thinking in how we solve problems as we are committed to the people we serve. Claude Corps applies AI talent where it makes an immediate difference, from understanding donors and distribution forecasting to turning data into faster decisions for families counting on us. Scott Burns President and CEO , Montgomery County Food Bank Heartland Forward is committed to ensuring the 20 states in the middle of the country are prepared for an economy that’s changing because of artificial intelligence. From recent graduates to late-career professionals, having the AI skills and knowledge to advance economically has never been more important. Our partnership with Anthropic and Claude Corps helps ensure that the heartland workforce is setting the pace in harnessing the transformative power of AI for good. Krista Cupp Executive Vice President of Partnerships , Heartland Forward At RAINN, the time our team spends on technical infrastructure is time away from helping survivors. Claude Corps will help us expand our secure, private tools for survivors faster, so our staff can focus on what only humans can do: supporting victims in crisis. We aren’t replacing human connection. We’re protecting it. Bill Bondurant CTO , RAINN REEF is excited about the potential for AI to help scale ocean conservation, citizen science, and public engagement. As a grassroots nonprofit managing large marine datasets and conservation technology projects, Claude Corps gives us an opportunity to build tools and workflows that would otherwise be out of rea

articles.title: DXC integrates Claude into systems regulated industries rely on
articles.contentText: Announcements DXC will integrate Claude into the systems banks, airlines, and other regulated industries rely on Jun 11, 2026 We’re announcing a multi-year global alliance with DXC Technology, one of the world’s largest IT services companies. DXC will train tens of thousands of Claude-certified forward-deployed engineers (FDEs)—engineers embedded directly inside customer organizations—to bring Claude into the systems DXC operates for the world’s largest banks, airlines, insurers, manufacturers, and government agencies. These systems, which DXC has run for decades, handle the transactions, claims, and operations businesses depend on, under strict security and compliance requirements. Before bringing Claude to these businesses, DXC worked with Claude inside its own operations, comprising some 115,000 employees across 70 countries, with similarly stringent requirements—including collaborating with Claude to write more than 95% of the code for DXC OASIS, its new AI-native orchestration platform for managed services. DXC is also now part of the Claude Partner Network , our network of consulting and services firms that bring Claude to enterprise clients. Bringing Claude to DXC's own operations DXC worked with Claude in its own systems before rolling it out to clients. For example, in April 2026, DXC launched DXC OASIS, its tool for running customers' IT systems, where AI agents handle much of the routine work. Claude is now the default foundation model powering the platform’s agentic workflows. Claude was also the primary tool DXC used to build the platform. The firm estimates that Claude sped up software development by a factor of 10. More than 95% of the code was generated by Claude, then reviewed by software engineers. OASIS now serves over 50 DXC customers, and the firm plans to roll it out to customers around the world. “DXC helps the world’s largest banks, airlines, insurers, and government agencies put new technology to work,” said Paul Smith, Chief Commercial Officer, Anthropic. “They proved Claude inside their own operations first, under the same security and compliance requirements their customers face. Now we’re bringing Claude inside those environments together, industry by industry, with engineers who have already done it themselves.” Building a team of Claude-certified engineers For its FDE program, DXC will recruit engineers from its existing development teams and certify them through Anthropic Academy , our training and certification program for partner engineers. DXC has added its own curriculum on top of Anthropic Academy, with training specific to the mission-critical systems its customers operate. The alliance will begin in four areas where DXC already runs large operations for customers, and where Claude can do useful work today: Insurance: DXC will use Claude to provide agentic solutions and modernize core systems, taking into account each customer’s business context, operating model, and strategic needs. Modernization as a Service (MaaS): DXC is using Claude to help enterprise customers analyze, refactor, and modernize legacy codebases. Working with Claude will help them do so faster and with greater accuracy than would have been possible with traditional approaches. Cybersecurity : For OASIS, DXC is developing an always-on security engineer subagent, built on Claude Security, that will be deployed across its security operations centers (SOCs). Application services: Finally, DXC is using Claude to develop OASIS agents that embed Claude directly into the application maintenance and management environments DXC operates for its enterprise customers. “For more than 50 years, DXC and the companies it was built from run the systems that run the world,” said Raul Fernandez, President and CEO, DXC Technology. “We know what it takes to deliver in these environments. This alliance with Anthropic combines trust and experience with the most advanced AI technology available, and gives our customers something they cannot get anywhere else. We are already using Claude across our own operations and our new OASIS platform. Now we are scaling that capability directly into the mission-critical technology systems we run for our customers. This is a defining moment for DXC and for the industry.” To learn more about the Claude Partner Network, visit anthropic.com/partners .
